summary: This PR refactors `HTTPDigestAuth` to use `threading.local` for per-thread state, making it safe for concurrent use. While the intent is sound, several correctness issues exist around uninitialized or mis-initialized thread-local state (particularly `num_401_calls` being set to `None` instead of `1`, and missing guards in hook handlers), the threaded test has structural weaknesses (silent skipping, incomplete assertions), and there are style/coverage gaps. The critical and major issues should be addressed before merging.

findings.title: num_401_calls initialized to None instead of 1, causing TypeError on comparison in handle_401
findings.detail: In `init_per_thread_state`, `self.tl.num_401_calls` is set to `None` (line 78). In `handle_401`, the code reads and compares this value with `< 2` (line 180) and later increments it with `+= 1` (line 182). If `handle_401` fires before `__call__` sets `num_401_calls = 1` (line 221) — or if the thread-local is initialized via `init_per_thread_state` but `__call__` hasn't run yet — the comparison `None < 2` raises a `TypeError` in Python 3 (and produces undefined ordering in Python 2), and `None += 1` raises a `TypeError` unconditionally. The original code defensively used `getattr(self, 'num_401_calls', 1)` precisely to guard against this; both that guard and the `__init__` default of `1` have been removed without an equivalent replacement. Fix: initialize `self.tl.num_401_calls = 1` (not `None`) in `init_per_thread_state`.

findings.title: Threaded test silently skips and never asserts HTTP 200 status
findings.detail: The entire test body is wrapped in `if ThreadPool is not None`, meaning the test always reports as passing even when `ThreadPool` is unavailable — giving a false sense of coverage. Other tests in the file that require optional infrastructure use `pytest.mark.skipif` or `unittest.skipIf` to produce an explicit skip signal. Additionally, the test only asserts that the `Authorization` header contains `"auth"` but never asserts `r.status_code == 200`, leaving the case where all threads actually receive 401 responses undetected. Fix: use a proper skip decorator for the `ThreadPool` guard and add a status code assertion.

findings.title: No unit-level test for init_per_thread_state or per-thread attribute initialization
findings.detail: The new `init_per_thread_state` method and the per-thread `tl` attributes are only exercised indirectly through integration tests hitting a live server. There is no isolated test that: (a) calls `init_per_thread_state()` directly and verifies the correct initial values (especially `num_401_calls = 1`, not `None`), (b) verifies a second call on the same thread does NOT reinitialize, and (c) verifies a new thread gets a fresh, independent set of attributes. These cases are the core invariants of the thread-safety fix.

findings.title: handle_redirect and handle_401 access thread-local attributes without ensuring init_per_thread_state was called
findings.detail: `handle_redirect` (lines 165–168) directly assigns `self.tl.num_401_calls = 1` without calling `init_per_thread_state()`, leaving all other thread-local attributes (`pos`, `chal`, `last_nonce`, `nonce_count`) potentially absent. `handle_401` (lines 170–177) accesses `self.tl.pos` and `self.tl.num_401_calls` without calling `init_per_thread_state()` either. In any scenario where these hooks fire on a thread that has never executed `__call__` (e.g. internal redirect handling on a worker thread, or async/executor patterns), the missing attributes will raise `AttributeError`. Additionally, `handle_redirect` does not reset `last_nonce` or `chal`, which could allow digest credentials from one host to be reused after a redirect to a different host. Fix: call `init_per_thread_state()` at the top of both `handle_redirect` and `handle_401`, and consider clearing nonce/challenge state on cross-host redirects.

findings.title: handle_redirect resetting tl.num_401_calls is not covered by any test
findings.detail: The `handle_redirect` branch that resets `self.tl.num_401_calls = 1` is not exercised by any existing test — neither the threaded test nor the non-threaded digest tests follow a redirect through `handle_redirect`. There is no test that performs a redirect while using `HTTPDigestAuth` and asserts that `num_401_calls` is reset to `1` afterward.

findings.title: Opaque attribute name `tl` for thread-local storage
findings.detail: The new threading.local container is named `tl`, which is an unexplained abbreviation. The rest of the file uses full, descriptive attribute names. A name like `_thread_local` or `_local` would be more readable and self-documenting, and the leading underscore would signal that it is an implementation detail not intended for external access.

findings.title: init_per_thread_state should be a private method (underscore prefix)
findings.detail: `init_per_thread_state` is a purely internal initialization helper with no intended public interface. Python convention signals implementation-detail methods with a leading underscore (e.g. `_init_per_thread_state`). Without it, the method appears to be part of the public API, which is inconsistent with the intent and could confuse downstream users who subclass `HTTPDigestAuth`.

findings.title: Per-thread num_401_calls counter invariants lack threaded and sequential-reuse test coverage
findings.detail: Two important behavioral invariants of the per-thread counter have no test coverage: (1) two concurrent threads each independently reaching the `num_401_calls >= 2` guard (verifying isolation between threads, the core promise of the PR), and (2) sequential reuse of the same `HTTPDigestAuth` instance on the same thread after a failed auth left the counter above 1, verifying that `__call__` correctly resets it for the next request. The existing non-threaded `test_DIGESTAUTH_WRONG_HTTP_401_GET` only exercises the single-threaded path with the old instance-level attribute.

findings.title: Thread-local init guard permanently suppresses re-initialization; stale nonce state carried over between sessions
findings.detail: The `hasattr(self.tl, 'init')` guard in `init_per_thread_state` is set once and never cleared, meaning re-initialization is permanently suppressed for the lifetime of the thread. When the same `HTTPDigestAuth` object is reused across different sessions on the same thread (a documented pattern), stale `last_nonce`, `chal`, or `nonce_count` values from a previous session are silently carried over. This could cause the client to skip the 401 handshake and send a digest built with a server-rejected nonce, potentially leaking credentials or enabling replay if the server accepts old nonces. Consider clearing or resetting relevant state at the start of each `__call__` invocation rather than relying solely on the once-per-thread guard.

findings.title: num_401_calls reset to 1 occurs after hook registration, subtle ordering difference from original
findings.detail: Every call to `__call__` registers fresh `handle_401` and `handle_redirect` hooks and then sets `self.tl.num_401_calls = 1`. In the original code, `num_401_calls` started at `1` from `__init__` and was reset there, so there was no window between hook registration and counter initialization. In the new code, if a hook somehow fires in the narrow window between hook registration (line 219) and the reset (line 221), it will see the stale counter value from the previous request. While this is unlikely under normal synchronous use, it is a subtle ordering difference worth documenting or fixing by moving the reset before hook registration.

findings.title: Missing spaces around `=` in keyword argument assignment
findings.detail: `session.auth=auth` is missing spaces around the `=`. PEP 8 and the surrounding test code use spaces for regular assignments: `session.auth = auth`.

findings.title: Sentinel attribute `tl.init` is not descriptive
findings.detail: The flag `self.tl.init = True` / `hasattr(self.tl, 'init')` used to guard re-initialization would be clearer as `self.tl.initialized` or `self.tl._initialized`, making the intent immediately apparent without requiring the reader to infer it from context.

findings.title: Returning magic literal `1` from `do_request` serves no apparent purpose
findings.detail: `do_request` returns `1`, but the return value of `pool.map` is never used. This makes the reader wonder whether the constant has some significance. Either drop the `return` statement (implicitly returning `None` is fine for `pool.map`) or add a comment explaining why it exists.

findings.title: num_401_calls is None when handle_401 is first entered, causing TypeError on comparison
findings.detail: In `init_per_thread_state`, `self.tl.num_401_calls` is initialized to `None` (line 78). In `__call__`, it is set to `1` at line 221 — but only AFTER `r.register_hook('response', self.handle_401)` is registered at line 219. The `handle_401` method reads `self.tl.num_401_calls` at line 177 and then compares it with `< 2` at line 180. If `handle_401` is somehow invoked on a thread before `__call__` sets it to `1` (e.g. a redirect hook fires before the assignment), the value will be `None` and `None < 2` raises a `TypeError` in Python 3. More importantly, the original code initialised `num_401_calls = 1` in `__init__` and used `getattr(self, 'num_401_calls', 1)` as a safety fallback; both safety nets are now gone. The fix is to initialize `self.tl.num_401_calls = 1` (not `None`) in `init_per_thread_state`, mirroring the original default.

findings.title: handle_redirect accesses self.tl.num_401_calls without guaranteeing init_per_thread_state was called first
findings.detail: `handle_redirect` (line 165-168) directly writes `self.tl.num_401_calls = 1` without calling `init_per_thread_state()`. If a redirect response is processed on a thread that has never called `__call__` (and thus never called `init_per_thread_state`), the attribute will be set, but the other thread-local attributes (`pos`, `chal`, `last_nonce`, `nonce_count`) will be missing. A subsequent call to `handle_401` or `build_digest_header` on that same thread will then raise an `AttributeError`. `init_per_thread_state()` should be called at the top of both `handle_redirect` and `handle_401`.

findings.title: handle_401 accesses thread-local attributes without guaranteeing init_per_thread_state was called
findings.detail: Similarly to `handle_redirect`, `handle_401` directly accesses `self.tl.pos` (line 173) and `self.tl.num_401_calls` (line 177) without first calling `init_per_thread_state()`. In a scenario where a response hook fires on a different thread than the one that made the original request (possible with some async/executor patterns), these attributes will not exist and an `AttributeError` will be raised. The guard `init_per_thread_state` should be called at the beginning of `handle_401` as well.

findings.title: num_401_calls is reset to 1 unconditionally at the end of __call__, after hooks are already registered
findings.detail: Every time `__call__` runs, `self.tl.num_401_calls = 1` is set at line 221 — after the hooks are registered. Because hooks accumulate (each `__call__` registers an additional `handle_401` and `handle_redirect` hook on the prepared request), and `num_401_calls` is reset to 1 every time, a thread that reuses the auth object makes multiple calls and could trigger unexpected behavior. While this is consistent with the original design, it is worth noting the reset happens after hook registration in the new code, which is a subtle ordering difference from the original where `num_401_calls` started at 1 from `__init__`. No immediate bug under normal use, but could mask double-firing.

findings.title: Uninitialized num_401_calls can cause AttributeError or None arithmetic
findings.detail: In `handle_401`, `self.tl.num_401_calls` is read (line 177) and then incremented (`self.tl.num_401_calls += 1`, line 182) without any guarantee that `init_per_thread_state()` has been called first on this thread for *this particular response hook invocation*. If a thread somehow reaches the hook without having gone through `__call__` (e.g. the hook is registered on a redirected request spawned internally by requests on a different thread), `self.tl` will not have the `num_401_calls` attribute and the code will raise `AttributeError`. The previous code defensively used `getattr(self, 'num_401_calls', 1)` (removed at line 175) precisely for this reason. The new code removes that guard without adding an equivalent one for the thread-local case. Additionally, `init_per_thread_state` sets `self.tl.num_401_calls = None` (line 78), and if `__call__` is not reached before `handle_401`, the `None < 2` comparison will silently evaluate to True in Python 2 (unpredictable ordering) or raise a TypeError in Python 3.

findings.title: Thread-local init guard relies on a writable sentinel — not truly thread-safe for reuse across sessions
findings.detail: The `init_per_thread_state` method uses `hasattr(self.tl, 'init')` as a once-per-thread guard. Because `threading.local` storage is per-thread *and* per-object this is correct for a single `HTTPDigestAuth` instance, but `tl.init = True` permanently suppresses re-initialization for the lifetime of that thread. If the same `HTTPDigestAuth` object is reused across different sessions on the same thread (a documented usage pattern), stale `last_nonce`, `chal`, or `nonce_count` values from a previous session are silently carried over. This is not a direct security vulnerability, but stale nonce state could cause the client to skip the 401 handshake and send a digest built with a server-rejected nonce, potentially leaking credentials or enabling replay if the server accepts old nonces.

findings.title: handle_redirect resets num_401_calls without calling init_per_thread_state
findings.detail: `handle_redirect` writes directly to `self.tl.num_401_calls = 1` (line 168) without ensuring the thread-local namespace has been initialized. If this hook fires on a thread that has never executed `__call__` (unlikely but possible in edge cases involving internal redirect handling on worker threads), it will raise an `AttributeError`. More importantly, it does not reset other thread-local state such as `last_nonce` or `chal`, which could allow a redirect to a different host to reuse digest credentials from the previous host.

findings.title: Abbreviated/opaque attribute name `tl` for thread-local storage
findings.detail: The existing code uses full, descriptive attribute names (`last_nonce`, `nonce_count`, `chal`, `pos`, `num_401_calls`). The new attribute that holds all per-thread state is named `tl`, which is an opaque abbreviation. A name like `_thread_local` or `_local` would be more readable and self-documenting, consistent with the descriptive naming convention visible throughout the file.

findings.title: `init_per_thread_state` is a public method that is really an implementation detail
findings.detail: All other helper methods in `HTTPDigestAuth` that are not part of the public API follow the convention used by the rest of the codebase (`handle_redirect`, `handle_401`, `build_digest_header`). A method purely for internal initialization of thread-local state would be more consistent with the Python convention of prefixing implementation-detail methods with an underscore (e.g. `_init_per_thread_state`), signalling that it is not part of the intended public interface.

findings.title: Sentinel attribute named `init` is not descriptive
findings.detail: `self.tl.init` is used as a flag to record that per-thread state has been initialized. A name like `self.tl.initialized` or `self.tl._initialized` would make the intent immediately clear without having to read the surrounding logic.

findings.title: Missing space around `=` in keyword argument assignment
findings.detail: `session.auth=auth` is missing spaces around the `=`. All other assignments in the surrounding test code (and PEP 8, which the rest of the file follows) use spaces around `=` for regular assignments: `session.auth = auth`.

findings.title: Returning a magic literal `1` from `do_request` serves no apparent purpose
findings.detail: `do_request` returns `1` but the return value of `pool.map` is never used. Returning a constant makes the reader wonder whether the value has some significance. Either drop the `return` statement entirely (the function implicitly returns `None`, which is fine for `pool.map`) or add a comment explaining why it is needed, to be consistent with the straightforward style of the other test helpers.

findings.title: `if ThreadPool is not None` guard silently skips the test body
findings.detail: Other tests in the file that require optional infrastructure either skip unconditionally or use `pytest.mark.skipif` / `unittest.skipIf` to give an explicit skip message. Wrapping the actual assertions in an `if` block means the test always reports as passing even when `ThreadPool` is unavailable, giving a false sense of coverage. Existing skipping conventions in the file (e.g. `try/except ImportError` at the top followed by a conditional decorator) would be more consistent.

findings.title: init_per_thread_state() has no unit-level test
findings.detail: The new `init_per_thread_state` method and the per-thread `tl` attributes (last_nonce, nonce_count, chal, pos, num_401_calls) are only exercised indirectly through integration tests that hit a live httpbin server. There is no isolated/unit test that directly calls `init_per_thread_state()` and verifies that (a) the attributes are created with the correct initial values, (b) a second call on the same thread does NOT reinitialise them (the `if not hasattr(self.tl, 'init')` guard), and (c) a new thread gets a fresh set of attributes.

findings.title: handle_redirect resetting tl.num_401_calls is not tested
findings.detail: `handle_redirect` now resets `self.tl.num_401_calls = 1` on redirects. The existing `test_DIGESTAUTH_THREADED` and other digest tests do not exercise a redirect that passes through `handle_redirect`. There is no test that follows a redirect while using `HTTPDigestAuth` and then verifies that `tl.num_401_calls` is reset to 1, meaning this branch is untested.

findings.title: Threaded test is silently skipped when ThreadPool is unavailable and does not assert HTTP 200
findings.detail: The entire thread-safety test body (`pool.map(...)`) is guarded by `if ThreadPool is not None`. If `ThreadPool` is unavailable, no assertion runs and the test passes vacuously. Additionally, the test only asserts that the `Authorization` header contains `"auth"` but never asserts `r.status_code == 200`, leaving the case where all threads actually receive 401 responses undetected.

findings.title: handle_401 branch when num_401_calls >= 2 (infinite-loop guard) has no threaded coverage
findings.detail: The existing non-threaded tests for the 401-loop guard (`test_DIGESTAUTH_WRONG_HTTP_401_GET`) exercise the single-threaded path. With the move to `threading.local`, the counter is now per-thread; there is no test verifying that two threads each independently trip (and reset) the `num_401_calls >= 2` guard, which is the core invariant the PR is trying to protect.

findings.title: __call__ sets tl.num_401_calls=1 at end; no test verifies it resets correctly between sequential reuses on the same thread
findings.detail: The `__call__` method now sets `self.tl.num_401_calls = 1` unconditionally at the end (line 221). If the same `HTTPDigestAuth` instance is reused sequentially on the same thread after a previous failed auth left `num_401_calls > 1`, this line resets it. There is no explicit test that reuses the same auth object across multiple sequential requests on the same thread and checks that `num_401_calls` is correctly reset each time.

findings.title: num_401_calls initialized to None instead of 1, causing TypeError on comparison in handle_401
findings.detail: In `init_per_thread_state`, `self.tl.num_401_calls` is set to `None` (line 78). In `handle_401`, the code reads and compares this value with `< 2` (line 180) and later increments it with `+= 1` (line 182). If `handle_401` fires before `__call__` sets `num_401_calls = 1` (line 221) — or if the thread-local is initialized via `init_per_thread_state` but `__call__` hasn't run yet — the comparison `None < 2` raises a `TypeError` in Python 3 (and produces undefined ordering in Python 2), and `None += 1` raises a `TypeError` unconditionally. The original code defensively used `getattr(self, 'num_401_calls', 1)` precisely to guard against this; both that guard and the `__init__` default of `1` have been removed without an equivalent replacement. Fix: initialize `self.tl.num_401_calls = 1` (not `None`) in `init_per_thread_state`.

findings.detail: The entire test body is wrapped in `if ThreadPool is not None`, meaning the test always reports as passing even when `ThreadPool` is unavailable — giving a false sense of coverage. Other tests in the file that require optional infrastructure use `pytest.mark.skipif` or `unittest.skipIf` to produce an explicit skip signal.
